title: Disturbance Metrics
text: disturbanceMetrics() function used calculate metrics described Table 52 Environment Canada Scientific Assessment Inform Identification Critical Habitat Woodland Caribou (Rangifer tarandus caribou), Boreal Population, Canada 2011 Update. metrics included : * Fire: % non-overlapping fire * Anthro: % non-overlapping anthropogenic disturbance * Total_dist: % total non-overlapping fire anthropogenic disturbance * fire_excl_anthro: % fire overlapping anthropogenic disturbance disturbanceMetrics() uses several spatial data layers calculate percentage disturbance area: example data set loaded includes small area Nipigon caribou range use example. Disturbance data sets can converted polygons year disturbance time since disturbance using (reclassDist). example fire data polygons containing year disturbance converted presence absence raster cumulative disturbance past 40 years. disturbanceMetrics prepare data calculate disturbance metrics used predictor variables Johnson et al. (2020). function can run several different ways, simplest provide spatial objects input. disturbanceMetrics function returns S4 object class DisturbanceMetrics. access data.frame one column per metric use results function.  Multiple linear feature inputs can also provided vector form. See vignette("caribouHabitat", package = "caribouMetrics") data processing input options.
code: # load example data and classify cumulative natural disturbance landCoverD = rast(file.path(pthBase, "landCover.tif"))  natDistD <- sf::st_read(file.path(pthBase, "fireAFFES2020.shp")) %>%    reclassDist(endYr = 2020, numCumYrs = 40, template = landCoverD,                dateField = "FIRE_YEAR")   anthroDistD = rast(file.path(pthBase, "anthroDist.tif")) linFeatDras = rast(file.path(pthBase, "linFeatTif.tif")) projectPolyD = st_read(file.path(pthBase, "projectPoly.shp"), quiet = TRUE) linFeatDshp = st_read(file.path(pthBase, "roads.shp"), quiet = TRUE) roadsD = st_read(file.path(pthBase, "roads.shp"), quiet = TRUE) railD = st_read(file.path(pthBase, "rail.shp"), quiet = TRUE) utilitiesD = st_read(file.path(pthBase, "utilities.shp"), quiet = TRUE) disturbance <- disturbanceMetrics(   landCover=!is.na(landCoverD),   natDist = natDistD,   anthroDist = anthroDistD,   linFeat = linFeatDras,   projectPoly = projectPolyD ) str(disturbance, max.level = 2, give.attr = FALSE) #> Formal class 'DisturbanceMetrics' [package "caribouMetrics"] with 8 slots #>   ..@ landCover         :S4 class 'SpatRaster' [package "terra"] #>   ..@ natDist           :S4 class 'SpatRaster' [package "terra"] #>   ..@ anthroDist        :S4 class 'SpatRaster' [package "terra"] #>   ..@ linFeat           :List of 1 #>   ..@ projectPoly       :Classes 'sf' and 'data.frame':  1 obs. of  2 variables: #>   ..@ processedData     :S4 class 'SpatRaster' [package "terra"] #>   ..@ disturbanceMetrics:'data.frame':   1 obs. of  6 variables: #>   ..@ attributes        :List of 3 results(disturbance) #>   zone   Anthro     Fire Total_dist fire_excl_anthro FID #> 1    1 46.74151 1.726477   47.29954        0.5580347   0 plot(disturbance@processedData) disturbanceV <- disturbanceMetrics(   landCover=!is.na(landCoverD),   natDist = natDistD,   anthroDist = anthroDistD,   linFeat = list(roads = roadsD, rail = railD, utilities = utilitiesD),   projectPoly = projectPolyD ) plot(disturbanceV@processedData)

text: describe two-stage demographic model density dependence interannual variability following Johnson et. al. (2020) modifications noted Hughes et al. (2025) Dyson et al. (2022). Demographic rates vary disturbance estimated Johnson et. al. (2020). detailed description model provided Hughes et al. (2025) Section 2.4. demographicCoefficients() selects regression coefficient values standard errors desired model version (see popGrowthTableJohnsonECCC options) samples coefficients Gaussian distributions replicate population. Next demographicRates() used apply sampled coefficients disturbance covariates calculate expected recruitment survival according beta regression models estimated Johnson et al. (2020). population optionally assigned quantiles Beta error distributions survival recruitment. Using quantiles means population stay quantiles disturbance changes time, persistent variation recruitment survival among example populations. Finally, can use estimated demographic rates project population dynamics using simple model two age classes. Interannual variation survival recruitment modelled using truncated beta distributions.

what: Simple demographic projection for a single example landscape
title: Caribou Demography
text: simple case demographic projection multiple stochastic projections single landscape change time. First define disturbance scenario 40% anthropogenic disturbance 2% fire disturbance. spatial data disturbance area interest use disturbanceMetrics() directly calculate disturbance. (See Disturbance Metrics vignette example). begin sampling coefficients 500 replicate populations using default Johnson et al. (2020) models “M1” “M4”. returned object list containing coefficients standard errors national model well sampled coefficients quantiles assigned . Next calculate sample demographic rates given sampled model coefficients disturbance metrics example landscape, setting returnSample = TRUE results returned contain row sample scenario. set initial population size sample population 100, project population dynamics 20 years using caribouPopGrowth function default parameter values. Anthropogenic disturbance high example landscape, projected population growth rate sample populations 1, uncertainty model means sample populations persist.
code: disturbance <- data.frame(Anthro = 40, fire_excl_anthro = 2) popGrowthPars <- demographicCoefficients(500)  head(popGrowthPars$coefSamples_Survival$coefSamples) #>       Intercept        Anthro Precision #> [1,] -0.1530718 -0.0008907556  60.65168 #> [2,] -0.1399809 -0.0007155384  61.65165 #> [3,] -0.1612743 -0.0007628660  68.57570 #> [4,] -0.1420441 -0.0007747503  52.81483 #> [5,] -0.1370847 -0.0009535161  52.68192 #> [6,] -0.1329182 -0.0008050787  60.32032 head(popGrowthPars$coefSamples_Survival$coefValues) #>    Intercept Anthro Precision #>        <num>  <num>     <num> #> 1:    -0.142 -8e-04  63.43724 head(popGrowthPars$coefSamples_Survival$coefStdErrs) #>      Intercept      Anthro Precision #>          <num>       <num>     <num> #> 1: 0.007908163 0.000127551  8.272731 head(popGrowthPars$coefSamples_Survival$quantiles) #> [1] 0.25345691 0.02880762 0.89123246 0.36007014 0.55806613 0.19063126 rateSamples <- demographicRates(   covTable = disturbance,   popGrowthPars = popGrowthPars,   ignorePrecision = FALSE,   returnSample = TRUE,   useQuantiles = FALSE)      rateSamples$N0 <- 100 demography <- cbind(rateSamples,                     caribouPopGrowth(N = rateSamples$N0,                                      numSteps = 20,                                      R_bar = rateSamples$R_bar,                                      S_bar = rateSamples$S_bar)) fds <- pivot_longer(demography, !(scnID:replicate) & !N0, names_to = "Metric",                     values_to = "Amount") d1 <- ggplot(fds, aes(x = as.factor(round(Anthro, 2)), y = Amount,                        colour = fire_excl_anthro)) +   geom_violin(alpha = 0.4, color = "black") +   geom_point(shape = 21, size = 2, position = position_jitterdodge()) +   facet_wrap(~Metric, scales = "free") +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   xlab("Anthro")  plot(d1)

what: Effects of disturbance on demographic rates
title: Caribou Demography
text: can project demographic rates range landscape conditions recreate figures 3 5 Johnson et al. (2020) see effects changing disturbance model behaviour. First create table disturbance scenarios across range different levels fire anthropogenic disturbance. sample coefficients default models M1 M4. sample 500 used calculate averages, sample 35 used show variability among populations. Next calculate demographic rates given sampled model coefficients. smaller sample set returnSample = TRUE results returned contain row sample scenario. Setting useQuantiles = TRUE assigns sample population quantile regression model error distributions survival recruitment, allows us see demographic rates change. larger sample set returnSample = FALSE result one row scenario includes summary statistics uncertainty across samples. twice, ignorePrecision = TRUE ignorePrecision = FALSE demonstrate effect considering variance among populations around National mean addition uncertainty coefficient estimates.
code: covTableSim <- expand.grid(Anthro = seq(0, 90, by = 2),                             fire_excl_anthro = seq(0, 70, by = 10))  covTableSim$Total_dist = covTableSim$Anthro + covTableSim$fire_excl_anthro popGrowthPars <- demographicCoefficients(   500,   modelVersion = "Johnson",   survivalModelNumber = "M1",   recruitmentModelNumber = "M4",   populationGrowthTable = popGrowthTableJohnsonECCC )  popGrowthParsSmall <- demographicCoefficients(   35,   modelVersion = "Johnson",   survivalModelNumber = "M1",   recruitmentModelNumber = "M4",   populationGrowthTable = popGrowthTableJohnsonECCC ) rateSamples <- demographicRates(   covTable = covTableSim,   popGrowthPars = popGrowthParsSmall,   ignorePrecision = FALSE,   returnSample = TRUE,   useQuantiles = TRUE )  rateSummaries <- demographicRates(   covTable = covTableSim,   popGrowthPars = popGrowthPars,   ignorePrecision = FALSE,   returnSample = FALSE,   useQuantiles = FALSE )  rateSummariesIgnorePrecision <- demographicRates(   covTable = covTableSim,   popGrowthPars = popGrowthPars,   ignorePrecision = TRUE,   returnSample = FALSE,   useQuantiles = FALSE )

what: Projection of population growth over time on a changing landscape
title: Caribou Demography
text: example, project 35 sample populations 50 years landscape anthropogenic disturbance footprint increasing 5% per decade. set interannualVar = FALSE, K = FALSE, probOption = "continuous" use simpler model without interannual variability, density dependence, discrete numbers animals, Stewart et al. 2023.
code: numTimesteps <- 5 stepLength <- 10 N0 <- 100 AnthroChange <- 5 #For illustration assume 5% increase in anthropogenic disturbance footprint each decade  # at each time,  sample demographic rates and project, save results pars <- data.frame(N0 = N0) for (t in 1:numTimesteps) {   covariates <- disturbance   covariates$Anthro <- covariates$Anthro + AnthroChange * (t - 1)    rateSamples <- demographicRates(     covTable = covariates,     popGrowthPars = popGrowthParsSmall,     ignorePrecision = FALSE,     returnSample = TRUE,     useQuantiles = TRUE   )   if (is.element("N", names(pars))) {     pars <- subset(pars, select = c(replicate, N))     names(pars)[names(pars) == "N"] <- "N0"   }   pars <- merge(pars, rateSamples)   pars <- cbind(pars,                  caribouPopGrowth(pars$N0,                                  R_bar = pars$R_bar, S_bar = pars$S_bar,                                  numSteps = stepLength, interannualVar = FALSE,                                   K = FALSE, probOption = "continuous"))    # add results to output set   fds <- subset(pars, select = c(replicate, Anthro, S_bar, R_bar, N, lambda))   fds$replicate <- as.numeric(gsub("V", "", fds$replicate))   names(fds) <- c("Replicate", "anthro", "survival", "recruitment", "N", "lambda")   fds <- pivot_longer(fds, !Replicate, names_to = "MetricTypeID", values_to = "Amount")   fds$Timestep <- t * stepLength   if (t == 1) {     popMetrics <- fds   } else {     popMetrics <- rbind(popMetrics, fds)   } }  popMetrics$MetricTypeID <- as.character(popMetrics$MetricTypeID) popMetrics$Replicate <- paste0("x", popMetrics$Replicate) popMetrics <- subset(popMetrics, !MetricTypeID == "N")

Limited local data: Observations 2014 2016. examine compare predictions unobserved years (2018 2021) add missing data. starting points analysis monitoring scenarios Bayesian Beta models informed local data prior knowledge national demographic-disturbance relationships. code: library(caribouMetrics) # use local version on local and installed on GH if(requireNamespace("devtools", quietly = TRUE)) devtools::load_all() library(bboudata) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)  figWidth <- 8 figHeight <- 10  #Note - set niters to 100 to run quickly when testing. Set to 1000 for complete results. niters <- 1000  useSaved <- T #option to skip slow step of fitting bboutools model surv_data = bboudata::bbousurv_a %>% filter(Year > 2010) surv_data_add = expand.grid(Year=seq(2017,2025),Month=seq(1:12),PopulationName=unique(surv_data$PopulationName)) surv_data=merge(surv_data,surv_data_add,all.x=T,all.y=T) surv_data$StartTotal[is.na(surv_data$StartTotal)]=1  recruit_data=bboudata::bbourecruit_a %>% filter(Year > 2010) recruit_data_add = expand.grid(Year=seq(2017,2025),PopulationName=unique(recruit_data$PopulationName)) recruit_data=merge(recruit_data,recruit_data_add,all.x=T,all.y=T) recruit_data$Month[is.na(recruit_data$Month)]=3;recruit_data$Day[is.na(recruit_data$Day)]=15  surv_dataNone <- surv_data %>% filter(Year > 2017) recruit_dataNone <- recruit_data %>% filter(Year > 2017)  surv_dataLimited <- surv_data %>% filter(Year > 2014) recruit_dataLimited <- recruit_data %>% filter(Year > 2014)  disturbance = data.frame(Year = unique(surv_data$Year), Anthro = 5, fire_excl_anthro = 5) disturbanceHigh <- data.frame(Year = disturbance$Year,Anthro = 80,fire_excl_anthro = 10)  betaLimited <- bbouMakeSummaryTable(surv_dataLimited, recruit_dataLimited, disturbance=disturbance,niters=niters) betaInformative <- bbouMakeSummaryTable(surv_data, recruit_data, disturbance=disturbance,niters=niters) betaLimitedHigh <- bbouMakeSummaryTable(surv_dataLimited, recruit_dataLimited, disturbance=disturbanceHigh,niters=niters)  simInformative <- getSimsInitial(betaInformative) simLimited <- getSimsInitial(betaLimited) simLimitedHigh <- getSimsInitial(betaLimitedHigh)

text: begin specifying monitoring scenario parameters, selecting example trajectory relatively low expected growth rate (.e. 1st percentile distribution mcmc samples initial model). example, specify 5 additional years monitoring, target 30 collars per year, 6 cows per collared cow composition surveys. simulate additional years monitoring, combine simulated observed data, reanalyze combined data. Methods simulating monitoring example trajectories described Hughes et al. (2025). Figure 1: Observed (2010-2017) simulated (2018-2022) survival data example trajectory relatively low expected growth rate (.e. 1st percentile distribution mcmc samples initial model). Figure 2: 95% prior predictive intervals Beta models without additional monitoring (5 years) example trajectory informative initial data (5 years), low disturbance, relatively low expected growth rate (Figure 1). Figure 3: 95% prior predictive intervals Beta models without additional monitoring (5 years) example trajectory limited initial data (2 years), low disturbance, relatively low expected growth rate (Figure 1). Figure 4: 95% prior predictive intervals Beta models without additional monitoring (5 years) example trajectory limited initial data (2 years), high disturbance, relatively low expected growth rate (Figure 1). examples, additional 5 years monitoring plausible low growth rate trajectory 5 intial years data provide additional clarity whether population likely decline future (Figure 2). contrast, additional 5 years monitoring substantially reduce uncertainty less initial data disturbance low (Figure 3). disturbance high, additional monitoring less reduce uncertainty clearer prior expectations national model even local data limited (Figure 4). Examining multiple example trajectories clarifies range possible outcomes additional monitoring.case true growth rate plausibly high (99th percentile distribution expected growth rate) 5 years additional monitoring clarifies population likely viable. case true growth rate plausibly low (1st percentile distribution expected growth rate) 5 years additional monitoring clarify whether population stable declining. Additional collars help distinguish cases one another, additional benefits information weighed additional costs. formal thorough analyses value additional monitoring can done examining outcomes larger representative sample plausible trajectories initial model (Hughes et al. (2025)). Figure 5: Outcomes additional monitoring vary among example trajectories (points), monitoring effort (collarCount). examples 5 years additional monitoring, limited initial data (2 years), low disturbance. Example trajectories 99th, 50th, 1st percentiles distribution expected growth rate initial model informed 2 years local data. : Hughes et al can also explore monitoring scenarios using simulated trajectories national model instead trajectories fitted Bayesian model.
code: scns=list() scns$lQuantile=0.01 #select example trajectories with low growth rate correlateRates = T #Force correlation among demographic rates to examine extreme cases scns$N0 = NA scns$projYears = 3 scns$curYear = max(simInformative$recruit_data$Year)- scns$projYears scns$obsYears = scns$curYear - min(simInformative$recruit_data$Year) scns$cowMult = 6 scns$collarCount = 30  trajectories <- subset(simInformative$samples,LambdaPercentile == round(scns$lQuantile*100)) trajectories <- subset(trajectories,Replicate==sample(unique(trajectories$Replicate),1))  oo <- simulateObservations(getScenarioDefaults(scns), trajectories,                            surv_data = simInformative$surv_data, recruit_data=simInformative$recruit_data) informativeMoreMonitoring <- caribouBayesianPM(surv_data = oo$simSurvObs, recruit_data = oo$simRecruitObs, disturbance=disturbance,niters=niters) out_tbls <- getOutputTables(informativeMoreMonitoring, simInitial = simInformative) typeLabsI <- c("More", "Informative")  #Use runScnSet to select trajectory, simulate observations, fit new model and summarize results for the case when initial data is limited. limitedMoreMonitoring = runScnSet(scns,simLimited,niters=niters)  typeLabsL <- c("More", "Limited")  limitedMoreHigh = runScnSet(scns,simLimitedHigh,niters=niters)  #TO DO: fix discrepancies between these two methods. #TO DO: change dot labels. "True simulated" should be "trajectory", and "observed" should be "observation". plotSurvivalSeries(oo$simSurvObs) #TO DO: fix data gap rec <- plotRes(out_tbls, "Recruitment", typeLabels = typeLabsI) surv <- plotRes(out_tbls, "Adult female survival", typeLabels = typeLabsI) lam <- plotRes(out_tbls, "Population growth rate", typeLabels = typeLabsI,                lowBound = 0, highBound = 1.5) rec / surv / lam rec <- plotRes(limitedMoreMonitoring, "Recruitment", typeLabels = typeLabsL) surv <- plotRes(limitedMoreMonitoring, "Adult female survival", typeLabels = typeLabsL) lam <- plotRes(limitedMoreMonitoring, "Population growth rate", typeLabels = typeLabsL,                lowBound = 0, highBound = 1.5) rec / surv / lam rec <- plotRes(limitedMoreHigh, "Recruitment", typeLabels = typeLabsL) surv <- plotRes(limitedMoreHigh, "Adult female survival", typeLabels = typeLabsL) lam <- plotRes(limitedMoreHigh, "Population growth rate", typeLabels = typeLabsL,                lowBound = 0, highBound = 1.5) rec / surv / lam #Add rows to the scenario table to examine multiple monitoring scenarios and example trajectories scnsMulti=scns;scnsMulti$lQuantile=NULL;scnsMulti$collarCount=NULL scnsMulti = merge(scnsMulti,expand.grid(lQuantile=c(0.01,0.5,0.99),collarCount=c(15,30)))  limitedMoreMulti = runScnSet(scnsMulti,simLimited,niters=niters)   lmmView <- subset(limitedMoreMulti$rr.summary.all,(Parameter=="Expected growth rate")&(Year==2021),select=c(lQuantile,collarCount,Mean,lower,upper,probViable)) base <- ggplot(lmmView,aes(x=collarCount,y=Mean,ymax=upper,ymin=lower,group=lQuantile,colour=probViable))+geom_point()+geom_errorbar()+ylab("Expected growth rate") base

what: Similarities and differences between the caribouMetrics Beta model and the bboutools model
title: Comparing the caribouMetrics Beta model to bboutools models
text: Hughes et al. (2025) described Beta model disturbance covariates informative priors analysis single boreal caribou population survival data aggregated year cows calves reported composition survey. align allow comparison models implemented bboutools (Dalgarno et al. 2025) extended Hughes et al. (2025) models follows: Allow analysis composition survey data bboutools form includes Yearlings, Bulls, UnknownAdults. See Analytic Methods Estimation Boreal Caribou Survival, Recruitment Population Growth details. Allow analysis monthly survival data bboutools form (e.g. bboudata::bbousurv_a). Allow calculation population growth rate without initial population size information. See Analytic Methods Estimation Boreal Caribou Survival, Recruitment Population Growth details. Allow analysis groups populations shared interannual variation. : add link bboutools documentation exists. Note bboutools includes number different model variants. Throughout document compare Bayesian model random effect year annual trend. brevity, refer “bbou” model. Key remaining differences caribouMetrics Beta model bbou model include: Beta model includes anthropogenic fire disturbance covariates. Beta model intercept disturbance covariate slope parameters informed national demographic-disturbance relationships. distribution interannual variation differs models. bbou model allows variation survival among months.

title: Comparing the caribouMetrics Beta model to bboutools models
text: compare Beta bbou models using three examples derived bboudata::bbousurv_a bboudata::bbourecruit_a data sets: local data: used examine compare prior predictions models. Informative local data: Observations 2010 2016. Limited local data: Observations 2014 2016. examine compare predictions unobserved years (2018 2021) add missing data.
code: library(caribouMetrics) # use local version on local and installed on GH if (requireNamespace("devtools", quietly = TRUE)) devtools::load_all() library(bboudata) library(bboutools) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)  figWidth <- 8 figHeight <- 10  useSaved <- T # option to skip slow step of fitting bboutools model bbouInformativeFile <- here::here("results/vignetteBbbouExample.rds") bbouLimitedFile <- here::here("results/vignetteBbbouExample2.rds") bbouPriorFile <- here::here("results/vignetteBbbouExample1.rds") bbouPriorNationalFile <- here::here("results/vignetteBbbouExample1p.rds") betaAnthroFile <- here::here("results/betaAnthroExample.rds")  surv_data <- bboudata::bbousurv_a %>% filter(Year > 2010) surv_data_add <- expand.grid(Year = seq(2017, 2022), Month = seq(1:12),                              PopulationName = unique(surv_data$PopulationName)) surv_data <- merge(surv_data, surv_data_add, all.x = TRUE, all.y = TRUE) surv_data$StartTotal[is.na(surv_data$StartTotal)] <- 1  recruit_data <- bboudata::bbourecruit_a %>% filter(Year > 2010) recruit_data_add <- expand.grid(Year = seq(2017, 2022), PopulationName = unique(recruit_data$PopulationName)) recruit_data <- merge(recruit_data, recruit_data_add, all.x = TRUE, all.y = TRUE) recruit_data$Month[is.na(recruit_data$Month)] <- 3 recruit_data$Day[is.na(recruit_data$Day)] <- 15  surv_dataNone <- surv_data %>% filter(Year > 2017) recruit_dataNone <- recruit_data %>% filter(Year > 2017)  surv_dataLimited <- surv_data %>% filter(Year > 2014) recruit_dataLimited <- recruit_data %>% filter(Year > 2014)

what: Models and Metrics of Boreal Caribou Demography and Habitat Selection
title: Models and Metrics of Boreal Caribou Demography and Habitat Selection
text: caribouMetrics R package provides implementations models Boreal woodland caribou (Rangifer tarandus caribou) demography described Hughes et al. (2025). include: national demographic model density dependence interannual variability follows Johnson et. al. (2020) modifications described Dyson et al. (2022). Demographic rates vary disturbance estimated Johnson et. al. (2020). Bayesian Beta model integrating prior information Johnson et al’s national analysis demographic-disturbance relationships available local demographic data reduce uncertainty population viability projections (Hughes et al. 2025). facilitate comparison Bayesian logistic models implemented bboutools R package (Dalgarno et al. 2025) caribouMetrics also allows use models. align bboutools note Hughes et al. (2025) model extended follows: Allow analysis composition survey data bboutools form includes Yearlings, Bulls, UnknownAdults. See Analytic Methods Estimation Boreal Caribou Survival, Recruitment Population Growth details. Allow analysis monthly survival data bboutools form (e.g. bboudata::bbousurv_a). Allow calculation population growth rate without initial population size information. See Analytic Methods Estimation Boreal Caribou Survival, Recruitment Population Growth details. Simulated example population trajectories can created national model fitted Bayesian models, combined simple observation model Bayesian population model explore monitoring scenarios show monitoring requirements depend landscape condition (Hughes et al. 2025). caribouMetrics also contains implementation Hornseth Rempel’s (2016) Ontario boreal caribou resource selection model described Dyson et al. (2022). Model implementation intended modular flexible, allowing reuse components variety contexts including projections cumulative effects disturbance climate change (e.g. Stewart et al. 2023).

code: library(caribouMetrics) #> Loading required package: nimble #> Warning: package 'nimble' was built under R version 4.3.3 #> nimble version 1.3.0 is loaded. #> For more information on NIMBLE and a User Manual, #> please visit https://R-nimble.org. #>  #> Note for advanced users who have written their own MCMC samplers: #>   As of version 0.13.0, NIMBLE's protocol for handling posterior #>   predictive nodes has changed in a way that could affect user-defined #>   samplers in some situations. Please see Section 15.5.1 of the User Manual. #>  #> Attaching package: 'nimble' #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     simulate # use local version on local and installed on GH if(requireNamespace("devtools", quietly = TRUE)) devtools::load_all() #> ℹ Loading caribouMetrics #> Registered S3 method overwritten by 'mcmcr': #>   method               from  #>   as.mcmc.list.mcarray rjags #> Warning: package 'testthat' was built under R version 4.3.3  pthBase <- system.file("extdata", package = "caribouMetrics")  # load example data landCoverD <- terra::rast(file.path(pthBase, "landCover.tif"))    # convert PLC classes to resource types used in the model  landCoverD <- reclassPLC(landCoverD) eskerDras <- terra::rast(file.path(pthBase, "eskerTif.tif")) eskerDshp <- sf::read_sf(file.path(pthBase, "esker.shp")) natDistD <- terra::rast(file.path(pthBase, "natDist.tif")) anthroDistD <-terra::rast(file.path(pthBase, "anthroDist.tif")) linFeatDras <- terra::rast(file.path(pthBase, "linFeatTif.tif")) projectPolyD <- sf::read_sf(file.path(pthBase, "projectPoly.shp"))  # calculate disturbance  disturb <- disturbanceMetrics(landCover = landCoverD,                               linFeat = linFeatDras,                                 natDist = natDistD,                               projectPoly = projectPolyD) #> cropping landCover to extent of projectPoly #> cropping linFeat to extent of projectPoly #> cropping natDist to extent of projectPoly #> buffering anthropogenic disturbance #> calculating disturbance metrics  disturb_tbl <- results(disturb)  # Calculate demographic rates demCoefs <- demographicCoefficients(replicates = 10)  demRates <- demographicRates(covTable = disturb_tbl,                              popGrowthPars = demCoefs) #> popGrowthPars contains quantiles so they are used instead of the defaults #> popGrowthPars contains quantiles so they are used instead of the defaults demRates #>   zone   Anthro     Fire Total_dist fire_excl_anthro FID     S_bar S_stdErr #> 1    1 39.97933 1.732936   40.56555        0.5862182   0 0.8478733 0.050644 #>     S_PIlow  S_PIhigh     R_bar   R_stdErr   R_PIlow  R_PIhigh #> 1 0.7690494 0.9321272 0.1813372 0.09859156 0.0538864 0.3760678  # Simulate population growth popGrow <- caribouPopGrowth(N = 2000, numSteps = 20, R_bar = demRates$R_bar,                              S_bar = demRates$S_bar)  popGrow #>     N0   lambda   lambdaE   N      R_t      X_t       S_t n_recruits #> 1 2000 0.945426 0.9247487 651 0.216816 0.108408 0.8930332         64 #>   surviving_adFemales #> 1                 587  # simulate caribou collar observations params <- getScenarioDefaults(   iAnthro = disturb_tbl$Anthro, iFire = disturb_tbl$Fire,   collarCount = 30, cowMult = 3,    obsAnthroSlope = 0, projAnthroSlope = 1, projYears = 10, obsYears = 10 ) simObs <- simulateObservations(params)  #devtools::load_all() pm <- caribouBayesianPM(simObs$simSurvObs, simObs$simRecruitObs,                           simObs$simDisturbance,                          # only set to speed up vignette. Normally keep defaults.                          niters=100) #> Compiling model graph #>    Resolving undeclared variables #>    Allocating nodes #> Graph information: #>    Observed stochastic nodes: 10 #>    Unobserved stochastic nodes: 33 #>    Total graph size: 449 #>  #> Initializing model #>  #> Compiling model graph #>    Resolving undeclared variables #>    Allocating nodes #> Graph information: #>    Observed stochastic nodes: 29 #>    Unobserved stochastic nodes: 70 #>    Total graph size: 505 #>  #> Initializing model  natSim <- getSimsInitial(simObs$simDisturbance) #> Warning: Setting expected survival S_bar to be between l_S and h_S.  pmTbls <- getOutputTables(pm, simInitial=natSim) plotRes(pmTbls, c("Recruitment", "Adult female survival")) #> $Recruitment #>  #> $`Adult female survival` # Calculate habitat RSF in Ontario's Churchill range carHab1 <- caribouHabitat(   landCover = landCoverD,   esker = eskerDras,    natDist = natDistD,    anthroDist = anthroDistD,    linFeat = linFeatDras,    projectPoly = projectPolyD,   caribouRange = "Churchill" ) #> cropping landCover to extent of projectPoly #> cropping linFeat to extent of projectPoly #> cropping natDist to extent of projectPoly #> cropping anthroDist to extent of projectPoly #> cropping esker to extent of projectPoly #> resampling linFeat to match landCover resolution #> resampling esker to match landCover resolution #> Applying moving window.  # plot the results plot(carHab1) #> tmap must be attached with library(tmap) to be used. what: caribouMetrics: Models and Metrics of Boreal Caribou Demography and Habitat Selection — caribouMetrics-package
title: caribouMetrics: Models and Metrics of Boreal Caribou Demography and Habitat Selection — caribouMetrics-package
text: caribouMetrics provides implementations several models Boreal woodland caribou demography habitat selection. national demographic model density dependence interannual variability follows [Johnson et. al. (2020)](doi:10.1111/1365-2664.13637) modifications described [Dyson et al. (2022)](https://doi.org/10.1101/2022.06.01.494350). Demographic rates vary disturbance estimated [Johnson et. al. (2020)](doi:10.1111/1365-2664.13637). package also includes Bayesian methods integrating prior information Johnson et al's national analysis demographic-disturbance relationships available local demographic data reduce uncertainty population viability projections ([Hughes et al. 2025](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecoinf.2025.103095)). national model can used simulate example population trajectories, combined simple observation model Bayesian population model show monitoring requirements depend landscape condition ([Hughes et al. 2025](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecoinf.2025.103095)). Finally, caribouMetrics contains implementation [Hornseth Rempel's (2016)](https://doi.org/10.1139/cjz-2015-0101) Ontario boreal caribou resource selection model described [Dyson et al. (2022)](https://doi.org/10.1101/2022.06.01.494350). Model implementation intended modular flexible, allowing reuse components variety contexts including projections cumulative effects disturbance climate change [(e.g. Stewart et al. 2023)](https://doi.org/10.1002/eap.2816).

title: Caribou demographic model — caribouPopGrowth
text: caribou demographic model density dependence interannual variability following Johnson et. al. (2020) modifications described Hughes et al. (2025) Dyson et al. (2022). Default parameter values give model Dyson et al. (2022). Set probOption = "matchJohnson2020" reproduce model used Johnson et al. 2020. Set probOption = "continuous", interannualVar = FALSE, K = FALSE reproduce simpler 2-stage demographic model without interannual variability, density dependence, discrete numbers animals used Stewart et al. (2023).

title: Get prior parameters for Bayesian beta population model — getPriors
text: modList named list modifiers use change priors. modifier supplied corresponding argument ignored. survivalModelNumber, recruitmentModelNumber character. model number use see popGrowthTableJohnsonECCC options. rAnthroSlopeSE Standard deviation effect disturbance recruitment. rFireSlopeSE Standard deviation effect fire recruitment. sAnthroSlopeSE Standard deviation effect disturbance survival. sIntSE Standard deviation survival intercept. sNuMin, sNuMax Uniform prior coefficient variation among years. rIntSE Standard deviation recruitment intercept. rNuMin, rNuMax Uniform prior coefficient variation among years. qMin number 0, 1. Minimum ratio bulls cows composition survey groups. qMax number 0, 1. Maximum ratio bulls cows composition survey groups. uMin number 0, 1. Minimum probability misidentifying young bulls adult females vice versa composition survey. uMax number 0, 1. Maximum probability misidentifying young bulls adult females vice versa composition survey. zMin number 0, 1. Minimum probability missing calves composition survey. zMax number 0, 1. Maximum probability missing calves composition survey. cowMult number. apparent number adult females per collared animal composition survey. populationGrowthTable data.frame.popGrowthTableJohnsonECCC included package used cases. custom table model coefficients standard errors confidence intervals can provided must match column names popGrowthTableJohnsonECCC. table contain standard error calculated confidence interval. modelVersion character. model version use. Currently option "Johnson" model used Johnson et. al. (2020), additional options may added future. returnValues logical. Default TRUE. FALSE returns strings values showing initial values modifier ie "0.9 * 1.05"

Maximum probability missing calves composition survey. cowMult number >= 1. apparent number adult females per collared animal composition survey. Set NA use cowCount. collarInterval number. Optional. Number years collar deployments. missing assumed every year cowCount Optional. used runScnSet() set number cows per year recruitment survey collarCount number >= 1. target number collars active year. Set NA use freqStartsPerYear simulateObservations() startYear year. First year observation period. Optional, provided calculated curYear obsYears interannualVar list logical. title: Load Spatial Input Data — loadSpatialInputs
text: projectPoly character sf. polygon delineating study area. refRast character raster. raster used template rasters must align (see alttemplate). inputsList list. named list inputs either spatial objects file names spatial objects. code: # create example rasters lc <- terra::rast(xmin = 0, xmax = 25000, ymin = 0, ymax = 25000,                       resolution = 250, crs = "EPSG:5070") lc[] <- 0 nd <- lc nd[1:30, 1:30] <- 1 ad <- lc ad[30:50, 3:50] <- 1 lc[] <- 1 lc[70:100, 70:100] <- 2  # create sf objects lf <- sf::st_as_sf(sf::st_sfc(list(sf::st_linestring(matrix(c(0, 0, 10000, 10000),                                                             ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE))),                               crs = 5070)) esk <- sf::st_as_sf(sf::st_sfc(list(sf::st_linestring(matrix(c(0, 10000, 10000, 0),                                                             ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE))),                               crs = 5070))   projPol <- sf::st_sf(sf::st_as_sfc(sf::st_bbox(ad)))  # prepare data res <- loadSpatialInputs(projectPoly = projPol, refRast = lc,                          inputsList = list(esker = esk, linFeat = lf, natDist = nd,                                            anthroDist = ad),                          convertToRast = c("esker", "linFeat")) #> cropping esker to extent of projectPoly #> cropping linFeat to extent of projectPoly

title: Reclassify natural disturbance and harvest layers — reclassDist
text: distYr sf object, SpatRaster RasterLayer. simple feature collection raster layer covering focal area including year disturbance time since disturbance well geometry (multipolygon) disturbance. endYr numeric default 0. Four digit year indicating latest year include distYr. 0 assume dateField values indicate time since disturbance. numCumYrs numeric. Number years endYr include. template SpatRaster RasterLayer. raster focal region, used template disturbance information projected. layer's dimensions determine dimensions output. recommended use landCover raster layer used caribouMetrics() disturbanceMetrics() ensure equal dimensions dateField character. Name column disturbance year/time since disturbance recorded.

title: Reclassify natural disturbance and harvest layers — reclassDist
text: Returns binary SpatRaster dimensions template input. Values 1 represent areas disturbance

title: Reclassify natural disturbance and harvest layers — reclassDist
code: library(sf) #> Linking to GEOS 3.12.1, GDAL 3.8.4, PROJ 9.4.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE # create template raster lc <- terra::rast(nrows = 10, ncols = 10, xmin = 0, xmax = 10, ymin = 0,                   ymax = 10, crs = "EPSG:5070")  # create fire polygons  corners <- matrix(c(0,0,10,10,5, 0,10,10,0,5), ncol = 2)  fireYr <- st_sf(FIRE_YEAR = c(1990, 2000, 2009, 2015),                      geometry = st_sfc(st_polygon(list(corners[c(1,2,5, 1),])),                                        st_polygon(list(corners[c(2,3,5, 2),])),                                        st_polygon(list(corners[c(3,4,5, 3),])),                                        st_polygon(list(corners[c(4,1,5, 4),]))))  fireYr <- st_set_crs(fireYr, 5070)  # three polygons should be considered disturbed (1) but the 2015 polygon should # not (0) cumFirePresence <- reclassDist(fireYr,                                endYr = 2010,                                numCumYrs = 40,                                template = lc,                                dateField = "FIRE_YEAR")  plot(cumFirePresence)  #> Error in plot.xy(xy, type, ...): invalid type passed to graphics function  # with time since disturbance fireYr$FIRE_YEAR <- c(50, 15, 10, 20) cumFirePresence2 <- reclassDist(fireYr,                                endYr = 0,                                numCumYrs = 40,                                template = lc,                                dateField = "FIRE_YEAR")                                 plot(cumFirePresence2) #> Error in plot.xy(xy, type, ...): invalid type passed to graphics function

title: Extract results — results
code: # create example rasters for disturbance metrics lc <- terra::rast(nrows = 10, ncols = 10, xmin = 0, xmax = 10, ymin = 0,                   ymax = 10, crs = "EPSG:5070") nd <- lc nd[1:3, 1:3] <- 1 ad <- lc ad[3:5, 3:5] <- 1 lc[] <- 1  # create sf objects lf <- sf::st_as_sf(sf::st_sfc(list(sf::st_linestring(matrix(c(0, 0, 10, 10),                                                              ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE))),                               crs = 5070)) projPol <- sf::st_sf(sf::st_as_sfc(sf::st_bbox(ad)))  # calculate disturbance dm <- disturbanceMetrics(landCover = lc,                          linFeat = lf,                          natDist = nd,                          anthroDist = ad,                          projectPoly = projPol,                          padFocal = TRUE,                          bufferWidth = 1) #> buffering anthropogenic disturbance #> calculating disturbance metrics  # default is disturbance metrics table resDM <- results(dm)  # can get other slots as well resDMrasters <- results(dm, type = "processedData")

title: Simulate observations — simulateObservations
code: simulateObservations(   paramTable,   trajectories = NULL,   cowCounts = NULL,   freqStartsByYear = NULL,   collarNumYears = 4,   collarOffTime = 4,   collarOnTime = 4,   caribouYearStart = 4,   recSurveyMonth = 3,   recSurveyDay = 15,   distScen = NULL,   writeFilesDir = NULL,   surv_data = NULL,   recruit_data = NULL )

title: Simulate observations — simulateObservations
text: paramTable data.frame. Parameters simulations. See getScenarioDefaults() details. trajectories data.frame. Optional example demographic trajectory. NULL trajectory simulated national model. cowCounts data.frame. Optional. Number cows counted aerial surveys year. NULL, paramTable contains cowMult number cows survive calving based collar data multiplied cowMult determine number cows counted aerial surveys. paramTable contain cowMult paramTable$cowCount used set number cows counted aerial surveys year. data.frame provided must columns "Year"  "Cows". freqStartsByYear data.frame. Optional. Number collars deployed year. NULL paramTable$collarCount used target number collars year collars deployed topped number. data.frame provided must 2 columns "Year" "numStarts" "numStarts" absolute number collars deployed year. collarNumYears integer. Number years collar falls collarOffTime integer. Month collars fall . number 1 (January) 12 (December) collarOnTime integer. Month collars deployed. number 1 (January) 12 (December) caribouYearStart integer. first month year caribou. recSurveyMonth integer. month simulated recruitment surveys. recSurveyDay integer. day simulated recruitment surveys. distScen data.frame. Disturbance scenario. Must columns "Year", "Anthro", "fire_excl_anthro" containing year, percentage landscape covered anthropogenic disturbance buffered 500 m, percentage covered fire overlap anthropogenic disturbance. See disturbanceMetrics(). NULL disturbance scenario simulated based paramTable writeFilesDir character. NULL simSurvObs simRecruitObs results saved csv files directory provided surv_data data.frame. Optional existing survival data bboudata format. combined simulated data ... Otherwise ignored. recruit_data data.frame. Optional existing recruitment data bboudata format. combined simulated data ... Otherwise ignored.

title: Update an existing DisturbanceMetrics Object — updateDisturbance
code: # create example rasters lc <- terra::rast(xmin = 0, xmax = 10, ymin = 0, ymax = 10,                       ncols = 10, nrow = 10, crs = "EPSG:5070") nd <- lc nd[1:3, 1:3] <- 1 ad <- lc ad[3:5, 3:5] <- 1 lc[] <- 1  # create sf objects lf <- sf::st_as_sf(sf::st_sfc(list(sf::st_linestring(matrix(c(0, 0, 10, 10),                                                              ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE))),                               crs = 5070)) projPol <- sf::st_sf(sf::st_as_sfc(sf::st_bbox(ad)))  # calculate disturbance  dm <- disturbanceMetrics(landCover = lc,                          linFeat = lf,                          natDist = nd,                          anthroDist = ad,                          projectPoly = projPol,                          padFocal = TRUE,                          bufferWidth = 1) #> buffering anthropogenic disturbance #> calculating disturbance metrics                     # new linear features lf2 <- sf::st_as_sf(sf::st_sfc(list(sf::st_linestring(matrix(c(0, 0, 10, 10),                                                              ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)),                                     sf::st_linestring(matrix(c(0, 9, 9, 10),                                                              ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)),                                     sf::st_linestring(matrix(c(10, 0, 5, 5),                                                              ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE))),                               crs = 5070)) dm2 <- updateDisturbance(dm, newData = list(linFeat = lf2)) #> buffering anthropogenic disturbance #> calculating disturbance metrics
